era_context: The Late Roman Empire in its final century of unified rule. The Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire under Theodosius I was consolidating Christian orthodoxy and military control. The Western Empire faced increasing pressure from Germanic migrations and internal instability. In East Asia, the Northern Wei Dynasty (Tuoba) was expanding control over northern China. In Mesoamerica, Classic Maya civilization was in decline, with major centres experiencing abandonment.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (archaeological record sparse)

events.title: Theodosius I issues edict against pagan temples and sacrifices
events.description: Emperor Theodosius I issued a series of edicts (part of the broader Theodosian Code) prohibiting pagan religious practices, including temple sacrifices and worship. This represented a major escalation in Christian imperial policy, moving beyond earlier restrictions to active suppression. The edicts were enforced unevenly across the empire but marked a decisive shift toward Christian monopoly on state religion. This policy had profound consequences for the survival of classical pagan institutions and knowledge.

sources.name: Theodosian Code (Codex Theodosianus), compiled 438 CE but preserving earlier edicts
Ambrose of Milan, Letters and sermons
Zosimus, New History (written early 6th century, hostile to Christianity)

events.certainty_note: The exact dating of Theodosius's anti-pagan edicts is debated. Some scholars place key legislation in 391–392 CE rather than 385. The Theodosian Code preserves the text but was compiled decades later. Ambrose's letters confirm pressure on pagan practices in this period.

events.title: Execution of Priscillian and followers in Hispania
events.description: Priscillian, a Christian bishop and ascetic reformer from Hispania, was executed by Roman authorities in Trier on charges of heresy and sorcery. Priscillian had advocated strict asceticism and challenged episcopal authority, attracting followers but also fierce opposition from mainstream bishops. His execution marked the first use of imperial power to execute a Christian for doctrinal reasons—a precedent that would shape religious persecution for centuries. The case revealed deep divisions within the Christian church over authority and practice.

sources.name: Sulpicius Severus, Chronicle (written early 5th century, near-contemporary account)
Augustine, Against the Priscillianists (later polemical work)

events.certainty_note: The execution is well-attested in near-contemporary sources. The exact charges and theological details are debated, but the fact and approximate date (385 CE) are secure.

events.title: Northern Wei Dynasty consolidates control over northern China
events.description: The Northern Wei (Tuoba) Dynasty, founded by the Xiongnu-descended Tuoba people, continued its consolidation of power over northern China. Under Emperor Xiaowen's predecessors, the Wei expanded territorial control and began the process of sinicization—adopting Chinese administrative practices, language, and culture. By 385, the Wei controlled much of the Yellow River basin and was emerging as a major power that would eventually unify northern China. This period saw significant cultural synthesis between nomadic and Chinese traditions.
key_figures: Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei (r. 471–499, but dynasty established earlier)
Tuoba Hong (regent, active in this period)

sources.name: Wei Shu (History of the Wei Dynasty), compiled 554 CE
Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled 11th century
Archaeological evidence from Datong and Luoyang sites

events.certainty_note: The broad trajectory of Northern Wei expansion is well-established. Specific events in 385 are less clearly documented in surviving sources. Dating relies on later compilations.

events.title: Alaric I becomes leader of the Visigoths
events.description: Alaric I, a Visigothic nobleman of mixed Roman and Gothic descent, rose to leadership of the Visigothic people in the Balkans. Alaric had served in the Roman army but increasingly led Gothic resistance to Roman authority. His rise marked the beginning of a process that would culminate in the sack of Rome in 410 CE. Alaric's leadership represented a shift from accommodation to confrontation between Germanic peoples and the Roman state, driven by disputes over settlement rights, military pay, and autonomy.

sources.name: Ammianus Marcellinus, Roman History (contemporary, but written earlier; later sources rely on him)
Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans

events.certainty_note: Alaric's exact rise to power is not precisely dated in sources. 385 is a conventional date, but some sources place his emergence as a leader in the 390s. The chronology depends on later compilations.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Theodosius's anti-pagan edicts is contested. Some modern scholars argue that the most severe legislation (CTh 16.10.10) belongs to 391–392 rather than 385, making the significance of 385 as a turning point less clear. Additionally, the extent to which these edicts were actually enforced across the empire is debated—pagan practices persisted in many regions for centuries. Priscillian's execution is sometimes interpreted not primarily as a heresy trial but as a political conflict between bishops, complicating its significance as a precedent for doctrinal persecution. Alaric's exact date of emergence is uncertain; some sources suggest the early 390s rather than 385.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 385 CE is dominated by Roman and Christian sources, creating a Western-centric bias. Eastern Roman (Byzantine) sources are limited. Chinese sources on Northern Wei are abundant but compiled centuries later. Germanic and other non-Roman perspectives are almost entirely absent from surviving documentation. The sources tend to emphasize religious and political elite events while obscuring economic, demographic, and cultural changes affecting ordinary people. Archaeological evidence is increasingly important for understanding this period but remains unevenly distributed geographically.

graph_edges.note: Theodosius's anti-pagan policy escalated in subsequent years
Priscillian's execution exemplified the enforcement of religious orthodoxy
Alaric's rise to power set in motion events leading to the sack of Rome
